definition: Central bank credibility as the 'master variable' for passthrough regimes is partially circular and overrated. Credibility is defined by outcomes (anchored expectations) then used to explain those same outcomes. No ex ante measure has predicted passthrough regimes. Japan (250% debt-to-GDP, no crisis) vs. Turkey (lower debt, serial crisis) is better explained by monetary sovereignty and FX liability structure.
evidence: No published ex ante credibility measure has predicted passthrough regime transitions
Japan (250% debt-to-GDP, no crisis) vs Turkey (much lower debt, serial crisis) explained by monetary sovereignty, not credibility
Brazil's transparent swap program vs Turkey's ad hoc selling produces systematically different ERPT outcomes
The Brazil-Turkey comparison cited across 5+ KB concepts suffers from omitted variable bias
open_questions: Can a multi-factor model incorporating monetary sovereignty, FX liability structure, and fiscal stance replace the credibility variable?
Is credibility simply a proxy for institutional quality and policy framework design?
